title: Detecting Eulerian Circuit in a Graph with Odd Vertices

statement.markdown: Given a graph with V vertices and E edges, a path that visits every edge exactly once and returns to the start vertex is called an Eulerian circuit. However, we need to account for odd vertices. An odd vertex is a vertex with an odd degree. If all vertices have even degrees, then a graph has an Eulerian circuit. If the graph has any odd vertices, then the graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if exactly one vertex has an odd degree. In any other case, the graph does not have an Eulerian circuit. You are given an adjacency list representation of a graph. Write a function to check if a graph has an Eulerian circuit or not.
statement.inputFormat: An array of adjacency lists representing the graph. Each index i represents a vertex, and the list at index i contains all vertices j such that there is an edge from vertex i to vertex j.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean value indicating whether the graph has an Eulerian circuit.

examples.explanation: This graph has an Eulerian circuit because it has two odd vertices.
This graph does not have an Eulerian circuit.

hints.text: First hint text: A graph has an Eulerian circuit if and only if every vertex has degree 0 or 2, or the graph has exactly one vertex with degree 1.

editorial.title: Editorial title: Eulerian Circuit Detection
editorial.intuition: Explanation: The problem can be solved by first calculating the degree of each node and checking if the degree of each node is 0 or 2. If not, we then check if the graph has exactly one vertex with an odd degree.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach summary: We need to check each node and count its degree. If a node has an odd degree, we need to check if the graph has exactly one node with an odd degree.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution details: We first calculate the degree of each node and check if the degree is 0 or 2. If not, we then check if the graph has exactly one vertex with an odd degree.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Correctness proof: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by checking the definition of an Eulerian circuit.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: We first calculate the degree of each node. Then we check if the degree is 0 or 2. If not, we then check if the graph has exactly one vertex with an odd degree.
commonMistakes: Mistake 1: Forgetting to check if the graph has exactly one vertex with an odd degree.
Mistake 2: Not handling the case where the graph has no edges.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool checkEulerianCircuit(vector<vector<int>>& graph); };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean checkEulerianCircuit(List<List<Integer>> graph); }

    def check_degree(self, graph):
starterCode.javascript: This solution attempts to solve the problem using a Map to store the degrees of each node, and then checks the count of odd vertices.

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool checkEulerianCircuit(vector<vector<int>>& graph) { int oddVertices = 0; for (auto vertex : graph) { int degree = vertex.size(); if (degree % 2 === 1) { oddVertices++; } } if (oddVertices === 0 || (oddVertices === 1 && degreeMap.get(graph.findIndex(v => v.length === 0)) !== undefined)) { return true; } else { return false; } } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean checkEulerianCircuit(List<List<Integer>> graph) { int oddVertices = 0; for (List<Integer> vertex : graph) { int degree = vertex.size(); if (degree % 2 === 1) { oddVertices++; } } if (oddVertices === 0 || (oddVertices === 1 && degreeMap.containsKey(graph.indexOf(vertex => vertex.size() === 0)))) { return true; } else { return false; } } }
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function checkEulerianCircuit(graph) { let oddVertices = 0; for (let vertex of graph) { let degree = vertex.length; if (degree % 2 === 1) { oddVertices++; } } if (oddVertices === 0 || (oddVertices === 1 && degreeMap.has(graph.findIndex(v => v.length === 0)))) { return true; } else { return false; } }
